event: Early modern humans inhabit Callao Cave in Luzon, evidenced by fossil remains later attributed to the species Homo luzonensis.
Austronesian-speaking peoples begin migrating into the archipelago, bringing agriculture, pottery, and seafaring technology.
Metal Age cultures emerge, with the development of jade ornaments, trade networks, and early ironworking across the islands.
The Laguna Copperplate Inscription, the earliest known written document in the Philippines, records a debt settlement and reflects ties to Javanese and Indic culture.
Chinese records mention the polity of Ma-i, an early trading state likely centered in Mindoro or Bay, sending envoys to Song China.
Chinese official Zhao Rugua describes the trading communities of Ma-i and the Visayan islands in his work Zhu Fan Zhi.
Arab missionary Karim ul-Makhdum introduces Islam to Sulu, founding one of the archipelago's first mosques.
The Sultanate of Sulu is established under Sharif ul-Hashim, becoming a major Islamic political and trading power.
The Sultanate of Maguindanao consolidates power in Mindanao under Islamic rule and expanding trade.
Ferdinand Magellan reaches the islands during the first circumnavigation and is killed by Lapulapu's forces at the Battle of Mactan.
Spanish explorer Ruy Lopez de Villalobos names the islands Las Islas Filipinas in honor of Prince Philip of Spain.
Miguel Lopez de Legazpi establishes the first permanent Spanish settlement at Cebu, beginning Spanish colonization.
Legazpi founds Manila as the capital of the Spanish East Indies after defeating the local ruler Rajah Sulayman.
The Manila Galleon trade begins, linking Manila and Acapulco and integrating the Philippines into global commerce for 250 years.
British forces capture Manila during the Seven Years' War, occupying the city until its return to Spain in 1764.
Governor-General Jose Basco establishes the tobacco monopoly, reshaping the colonial economy of Luzon.
Manila is formally opened to world trade, accelerating the growth of cash-crop agriculture and a Filipino merchant class.
The Cavite Mutiny leads to the execution of priests Gomez, Burgos, and Zamora, galvanizing Filipino nationalist sentiment.
Jose Rizal publishes the novel Noli Me Tangere, exposing colonial abuses and inspiring the reform movement.
Andres Bonifacio founds the Katipunan, a secret revolutionary society dedicated to independence from Spain.
The Philippine Revolution erupts against Spanish rule, and Jose Rizal is executed by firing squad in Manila.
Emilio Aguinaldo proclaims independence at Kawit, but the Treaty of Paris cedes the islands from Spain to the United States.
The First Philippine Republic is inaugurated and the Philippine-American War breaks out against U.S. annexation.
Aguinaldo is captured by American forces, and the United States establishes civil government under William Howard Taft.
The Philippine-American War is officially declared over, with U.S. colonial rule consolidated across most of the archipelago.
The Jones Act pledges eventual independence and creates an elected Philippine Legislature.
The Commonwealth of the Philippines is established with Manuel Quezon as president, transitioning toward full sovereignty.
Japan invades the Philippines shortly after Pearl Harbor, beginning a brutal occupation during World War II.
American and Filipino forces surrender at Bataan, leading to the deadly Bataan Death March under Japanese control.
General Douglas MacArthur returns at Leyte, launching the campaign to liberate the islands from Japanese occupation.
The Battle of Manila devastates the capital as Allied and Filipino forces retake the city from Japanese troops.
The Philippines gains full independence from the United States on July 4, with Manuel Roxas as the first president of the republic.
The Military Bases Agreement grants the United States long-term use of Clark Air Base and Subic Bay Naval Base.
Ferdinand Marcos is elected president, beginning a tenure that would extend through authoritarian rule.
President Marcos declares martial law, suspending civil liberties and consolidating dictatorial power.
Marcos formally lifts martial law while retaining sweeping powers under a new constitution.
Opposition leader Benigno Aquino Jr. is assassinated at Manila airport, igniting mass outrage against the regime.
The People Power Revolution peacefully topples Marcos, and Corazon Aquino becomes president.
A new democratic constitution is ratified, restoring the bicameral Congress and presidential term limits.
Mount Pinatubo erupts catastrophically, and the Senate votes to end the U.S. military bases agreement.
The last U.S. forces withdraw from Subic Bay, ending nearly a century of American military presence.
The second People Power uprising ousts President Joseph Estrada, elevating Gloria Macapagal Arroyo to the presidency.
Super Typhoon Haiyan (Yolanda) devastates the central Philippines, killing thousands in one of the strongest landfalling storms recorded.
Rodrigo Duterte becomes president and launches a deadly anti-drug campaign, while a UN tribunal rejects China's claims in the South China Sea.
The Battle of Marawi sees government forces retake the city from Islamic State-aligned militants after months of fighting.
The Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao is established following a peace plebiscite.
Ferdinand Marcos Jr., son of the former dictator, wins the presidency in a landslide election.
